speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. The date is January 14, 1996. The survivor is Mark Bader. The interviewer is Merle Goldberg. The city, Century City. The state, California. The country, United States of America. And the language, English. Where did you live before you lived here?

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Merle Goldberg, and today is January 14, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Mark Bader in Century City, California, United States of America. And I will be conducting the interview in the English language.

speaker: interviewer
text: To begin with, can you tell me your full name as you are called today, and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mark, M-A-R-K, Bader, B-A-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you also tell me the name you were given at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth I was given the name of Moses. In Polish, it's [? Moises. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Bader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bader.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't change your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Bader was always my name and still is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were growing up, did you have any terms of endearment or loving names that anybody called you, either your family or your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they called me [? Moisela. ?] They called me [? Moniac ?]. And they-- they most-- mostly these two names. And-- and my real name, which was always [? Moniac ?], but in cheder I was called by my Yiddish name. And in-- in school I was called by my given name, Polish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said [? Moniac ?]. Was that a polish name or a Jewish name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a Polish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on your birth certificate in Poland, would it have been-- what-- what would it have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that on the birth certificate would probably be [? Moises ?] Bader.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what city and country you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the city of Oswiecim, which during the war was renamed by the Germans to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you need to tell us the country as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were born in Oswiecim, was it part of Poland? You know how the borders changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the borders changed quite often. During my time it was-- belonged to Poland. However, my father was a-- was living in the same area, [INAUDIBLE], which is just maybe 30 to 35 kilometers away from Oswiecim. And he was, during World War I, he was in the Austrian army. He was an officer in the Austrian army. So it interchanged quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me your full birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is April 25, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we've gotten-- this-- this is what we do just at the beginning of the interview. You know as we talked on the telephone, and even just before we began, you were telling me how long that your family has-- had been in Poland for several hundred years that you know of. So I was wondering if perhaps you could tell me some history of your family and as much as you can about your grandparents, great-grandparents, great-great-great-grandparents, when they settled.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- since I lived in Oswiecim, this was the birthplace of my mother, which dates back to the-- the-- the maiden name was Enoch, E-N-O-C-H. And the Enoch family dates back to the 16th century in the city of Oswiecim, which at that time must have been Austria. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] During-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I was a-- a little boy, when I went to school and to cheder, Oswiecim was a peaceful little town. We were quite a few-- I had quite a few friends with a large family, lot of cousins, uncles and aunts. And it was a tranquil type of a community, even so that we always felt that the anti-Semitism was always present, from-- coming from the Poles, from non-Jew Poles. And-- but we didn't have much trouble with it because they-- everyone of the city was-- knew the name Enoch because of-- we were-- since it was a very old, established name. And also my grandfather had a hardware store in-- in center of town, which was right in the main centrum, in the main square. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I used to horse around quite a bit with my younger brother, and we were always very happy and-- and disruptive, to the point where my mother named me Haman. And every time I'm used to come back from school, or from cheder, she says-- she used to say, run for your life, Haman is coming. Unfortunately, my younger brother was killed in Auschwitz. He never survived the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before we actually talk about the Holocaust itself, I want to continue talking about those-- your earlier-- the earlier part of your life. You also said the other part of your family dated back many centuries. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The 17-- my-- the Bader family, which actually in Poland it was pronounced 'Bah-der.' They-- they dated back to the 17th century in Wadowice My grandfather Jacob and Tamara Bader were my grandparents. I had quite a few uncles and aunts. There were like, I think, 10 children, many cousins and uncles. A big family. And I used to visit Wadowice quite often, especially during vacation time when school was out. We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at home, we were six children. And we were a very happy bunch. We always [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] waited for the-- for Sabbath, for Shabbos, for the Saturday, because my mother used to bake quite a few cakes, various types of cakes like streuselkuchen, and moon cake, cheesecake, and maybe four or five different cakes. And every Saturday morning, we would eat in bed with-- so we would have the cakes, variety of cakes, and we used to gorge ourselves on it with milk. And then I used to, with my father, to-- to the temple. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a beautiful temple in-- in the city of Oswiecim, which the Germans destroyed, I think. It must have been in 1940 or maybe beginning of '41, because when I became bar mitzvah, I remember the temple was no longer there. They burned it one day. And my bar mitzvah took place in-- in a basement, when people were standing guard to make sure that the Germans would not find us, because it was against the law to congregate, or to have a group of people together. So I remember that I worked very hard to do a good job, but it-- it was-- it was in a-- in-- in the basement of the-- of my grandfather's house. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The-- we-- most of the family lived in my grandfather's building. Each child used to have their own apartment, and own their own apartment. It was a-- a large complex of buildings like a square itself. There was a-- it was on the main square of the town, but inside the complex there was a little square. That-- because the building was like a-- a quadrangle building with apartments on the second floor. Two-story building. And-- and stores on the first floor. So we used to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't too far from the temple, so we used to walk every Friday night, Saturday morning, and during high holidays or-- to the temple. My grandfather used to wear a top hat. And-- and everyone-- he purposefully walk-- walked diagonally through the main square, so that everyone would bow and-- and hold their hat and say, Good Shabbat, Mr. Enoch. Good Shabbat. And he was well-known in the community, and was also a big man, and-- and quite a showman. So-- In the temple, they used to sit on the western wall, only he and his son, which lived in the same building. And-- and the son-in-laws, and-- like my father and other husbands from-- of my aunts. It really was a very nice little Jewish community. There was quite a few Jewish people that lived in Oswiecim. And this-- unfortunately in 1941, this-- the community stopped to exist. We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother and I used to be very close. My younger brother and I used to be very close. And we would [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] horse around as much as we could, all day long. But we did study quite hard as well, because we went to Polish school in the morning, to the cheder in afternoon, and my parents insisted that we do our homework. So we played hard and we also studied hard. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When the war broke out, the-- the Germans occupied Poland in I believe September of 1939. They didn't have far to go to find Oswiecim because we were right very close to the German border. And a few weeks later, they renamed the main square where I lived to Adolf Hitler's Platz. And the town was renamed back to Auschwitz, which at one time, I believe, must have been Auschwitz, the name of Auschwitz during the time when-- when Auschwitz was part of Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before we get-- I keep taking you back. I was wondering perhaps if you haven't told me the names of your brothers and sisters, starting with-- or-- or your parents. You need for me to tell-- tell me what everyone's names were, and what your parents did, what kind of business your family was in.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six siblings. My oldest brother, his name is Herman. And my sister, Fela. My brother Abraham, which is Romek. Unfortunately, he was killed in the-- in the last few days of the-- just before the liberation, on a death march. And my sister Regina, my younger brother [? Sheik ?], his name was Joshua. [? Sheik ?] was his Polish name. Or that's what they called him, mostly. And so we were a large family. We-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] miraculously, the four of us made it. The rest, my father, my mother, and my youngest brother was killed in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell me about your parents a little? Their names, what they did, where they worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Isaac, [? Yitzva ?]. And my mother's name Rachel. My mother was mainly involved with the children and the household. And my father was in the lumber business. I remember as a little boy, my father used to cut lumber in the-- in the Carpath mountains in-- in Poland. And they used to slide down the hills, the lumber, as they-- and every piece of lumber used to have a stamp which was stamped with a-- by a-- with a hammer, which was IB. And the lumber was then channeled into the lumber mill, and cut to various type of lumber, for construction, for paper pulp, for telephone and electric poles, as well as lumber that used to support the coal mines, special type of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sizes of lumber.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to play many times in the-- in the-- my brother and I used to play around railroad, because everything was shipped by railroad. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but this was mainly in-- in the mountain areas. So when we used to go there on vacation, or sometimes during the summer time, we would go there. Speaking of playing, what kind of toys did you play with? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we really didn't have too many toys, but we played soccer. We played-- we used to make our own hockey sticks, and play hockey. Because there was really not too much traffic. Most traffic was pedestrian. And-- and-- or horses pulled wagons. There was very few cars in the city of Oswiecim as I remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since your family-- you indicated your family had been in this whole area for so many centuries, and you indicated that your-- there was such-- your grandfather was given such honor as he walked across the square, how would you describe your family? Was it well-to-do? Did you have servants? Did you-- like on Friday nights, did you invite poorer people to come and have a meal with you for tzedakah or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad you ask this question because my mother-- my grandmother name was Rose. And people used to tell me that she used to come around every holiday, like for-- for Passover, or Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and deliver food and-- and coals and-- to the-- to the poor people. And I-- people used to tell me that, because she died quite a while before I was born. So she-- people used to you say, your mother was really some person, because that's what most people remembered her by, especially [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jewish people in the community. So she was always busy helping out the poor people. We-- we had a-- we used to have a maid that lived with us, which I don't remember, because I was 12 years old when the war broke out. But we were-- we were very comfortable. They-- and-- and my grandfather was, I would say, well-- well-to-do. They owned farmland. They had-- he had a-- I believe what was the only hardware store in town. So it was-- they were well-off. And-- and-- and they were part of the Jewish-- they were active in the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rose was your grandmother, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you did say that she was your-- at one point, you said people used to tell me about my mother. I want to make sure that Rose--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose was my grandmother. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And-- but the other part of your family, which was your father's family--

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in Wadowice, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were-- did they have the same kind of lifestyle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were well-off, but not as well-to-do as the Enochs. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- they lived in Wadowice, and they were in the lumber business as well. They were 10 children. And many of the-- of my father's brothers, which were my uncles, used to pay-- my grandfather used to pay other people to go to the army. And then they-- they-- when it came down to my father, because the older brothers, they-- they couldn't do it anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they did all sorts of things, you know, to themselves. One cut his finger off, and one lit his-- one did something with the eyes, which from this he contracted tuberculosis, and he died-- died of that. So my father said he's not going to do anything. He-- he was drafted during World War I. And he served-- he was an officer in the cavalry. And they-- and he-- he also was three years in Siberia, because his regiment was storming Kiev.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to tell us stories of his experience, life experience. It was very interesting because he-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when they stormed Kiev, the-- the Russians were yelling, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], which means, run for your life, the Germans are coming. So instead of running away, they ran around them and circled them and them, and-- and took the whole regiment to prison in Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had-- he himself had quite an experience, and he was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he-- he was given some medal of distinction for his service. Which during the war, the Wehrmacht at the beginning, used to-- when my father used to show them the passport with the Polish Austrian military papers, they used to salute him and say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. You can go back home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which later was not the case, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the final result was that the-- and I experienced that my father was showing-- we were assembled again in the-- in the square and-- to do some work. We never knew what work they would do, where they will take us. And the officer in charge spit on the passport, threw it down on the-- his documents, threw it down, stepped on them. And he hit him twice in the face, and said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And this was pretty hard for my father to take. So that was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'd like to even-- I'd like to-- I'd like to stay back with you a little bit before. You know, you indicated how-- how it was on Shabbos at your house. I was wondering maybe if you could talk a little bit about, how-- were you a very, very religious family? Or a traditional family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would consider my family to be religious, very religious, but with the kind of experience that my father had in World War I, I'm sure that he could not keep kosher where he was. He probably was happy to get some food. So he was-- he shaved, and he was more-- he was very religious, but not fanatically so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- some of my uncles were extremely religious. They wore the black [NON-ENGLISH], what they called, with the shtreimel. The shtreimel, which was-- did you ever hear the expressions, shtreimel was like a round-- it was sort of cut to-- to-- It was a good looking hat. And that was-- I remember my uncles wearing those, especially the Baders. And they were extremely religious. My grandfather, Wilhelm Enoch, was more of a showman, but he was also very religious, but not to the point how my grandfather Jacob Bader was. He was extremely religious. He always stayed in the corner and praying very hard. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they both had beards, both of my grandfathers. But the-- Jacob Bader was truly, I would not call it fanatically, but Hasidic. And-- and he was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] praying to himself. My father, and-- just the opposite, like my grandfather Enoch would open the window Shabbas morning, and-- and-- and read out loud from-- from the-- from the portion of the five books of Moses that was for this particular Saturday. And so the whole community could see how well he knows it. So it was very-- they were both wonderful people, but there was-- I could see the difference. I was very close to my grandfather in Oswiecim, to Enoch, Wilhelm Enoch, because when he retired, he-- he raised pigeons. And-- and when I came home from school, I used to help him with-- with the pigeons. And he-- he knew, you know, the pigeons with the-- would sit down in-- in the middle of the little-- of the square in our building with food, and the pigeons would-- would come on over and sit down on his head and on the shoulders, and-- and he would feed them. And he knew every pigeon, and he knew exactly how many he had. It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's very unusual type of a hobby that he had. And they both lived to be old people. However, Not old enough because their life was cut down when the Germans sent them all to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're going to change the reel here. We're going to stop and change the reel. OK.
